doc_type: Complaint for Damages (Unlimited Civil Case) — California Superior Court, County of Sacramento
key_facts: Plaintiff BRENDAN NGEHSI NEWANFORBI is a parolee residing in Stockton, CA, currently supervised by CDCR's Division of Adult Parole Operations (DAPO), Stockton 2 GPS Parole Unit, Region N.

Plaintiff was released to parole on December 2, 2020, with a Controlling Discharge Date of December 2, 2030. He is a registered  (Penal Code §§ –.023) and participates in the  Management Program (SOMP) per Penal Code § 3008(d). His supervision category is 'SA' ( Active) with a CSRA Score of 2 (Moderate).

Under Penal Code § 3001(a)(2), Plaintiff was entitled to discharge from parole absent a lawful, individualized good-cause finding on or after December 2, 2021 — one year after his release. No good cause has ever been stated, documented, or demonstrated.
On November 12, 2025, Defendant DOE 1 (a CDCR/DAPO Parole Agent) prepared CDCR Form 1502-DR (Discharge Review Report) recommending Plaintiff's retention on parole.

On November 14, 2025, Defendant GARY NOGUCHI (Badge #7284), Unit Supervisor, reviewed and ratified the report. His written comments contain a copy-paste error referencing a different parolee — 'Eagan' — by name, proving no individualized review of Plaintiff's case occurred.

On November 19, 2025, Defendant KEELY DODD (Badge #6462), Parole Administrator, electronically signed and approved the Form 1502-DR, electing 'Discharge Denied/No Action' and writing 'Concur with the Unit Supervisor, continued parole supervision is warranted and recommended at this time.' This converted the field-level recommendations into a binding administrative decree.

On November 19, 2025, Defendant JOSEELYN ROJO assumed responsibility as Agent of Record but failed to serve the finalized report on Plaintiff — the 'Parolee Provided Copy (Date)' field on page 5 of the 1502-DR is blank, and neither the 'Mailed By' nor 'Delivered By' fields are completed.

The Form 1502-DR is procedurally void and defective in multiple ways: (a) it applies mutually exclusive statutory frameworks (Case Type PC 3000(b)(5)(B) allowing max 5–7 year parole while simultaneously applying a '10-year parole term' narrative); (b) it invokes a fabricated '6.5-year' minimum supervision requirement unsupported by any statute or regulation (an underground regulation prohibited by Gov. Code § 11340.5); (c) it applies a fabricated Policy 19-03 eligibility exclusion for '10-year parole terms' that does not exist in any published version of that policy; (d) it contains internally contradictory treatment characterizations, simultaneously commending Plaintiff for compliance while endorsing retention for non-compliance; (e) individual scores total 14 (falling in the 13–19 'Increase May be Warranted' band) but DOE 1 recommended 'Retain on Parole' without explanation; (f) it omits all mandatory Containment Team Meeting (CTM) documentation and CDCR Form 3043 required by 15 CCR § 3574(f); and (g) it uses the obsolete CDCR Form 1502-DR (Rev. 01/22) instead of the CDCR Form 3043 (Rev. 11/24) mandated by regulations adopted April 17, 2025.
Noguchi assessed Plaintiff as 'Stable 6/LSCMI 5,' placing him at Level 2 below-average risk, yet simultaneously endorsed retention — contradicting his own risk assessment.

Plaintiff has participated in the HOPE Psychotherapy, Inc. ('HOPE') treatment program continuously since December 2020. He has been assigned approximately seven different clinicians due to HOPE's extraordinary staff turnover, forcing repeated phase restarts. Despite this, he advanced to Core Phase, Block 1, Module 4 of 7. HOPE has never certified Plaintiff as having 'completed' treatment due to a blanket policy under which no parolee can complete treatment prior to discharge from parole regardless of clinical progress — a structurally impossible standard.

In re Dwyer (San Mateo Super. Ct. Case No. SC-080355A/HC-3020, Order Mar. 20, 2023) judicially condemned CDCR's structurally impossible treatment-completion standard. The April 17, 2025 amendments to 15 CCR § 3574 were enacted specifically to correct these deficiencies, explicitly providing that 'supervised persons may complete a  management program before discharge from parole.'

By November 2025, all Defendants had constructive knowledge — through Dwyer, Macomber, and the April 2025 regulatory amendments — that the practices applied to Plaintiff had been judicially condemned and legislatively repudiated.

Plaintiff has suffered documented economic losses exceeding $109,408 in lost CDL wages for 2022–2024, measured against his but-for earning capacity of $72,000 per year as a journeyman CDL holder (regular rate $33.35/hour; premium rate $50.03/hour). Losses continue to accrue at approximately $6,000 per month.

Plaintiff is subject to 24/7 GPS monitoring, curfew, geographic restrictions, mandatory  treatment (6–8 hours per week), warrantless searches, and employment limitations that prevent him from pursuing long-haul CDL employment. These conditions have persisted unlawfully from at least November 19, 2025 onward.

Plaintiff filed a government tort claim with the California Government Claims Program on March 21, 2026. The claim was deemed rejected by operation of law as of May 5, 2026 (45 days after presentment, per Gov. Code § 912.4).
Plaintiff filed this complaint on May 8, 2026, in pro per, in Sacramento Superior Court, with jury trial demanded.

Plaintiff's Controlling Discharge Date (nominal end of parole term)

arguments: FIRST CAUSE OF ACTION — Breach of Mandatory Duty (Gov. Code §§ 815.2, 815.6) against CDCR: Penal Code § 3001(a)(2) imposes a mandatory duty to discharge parolees after one year of continuous parole absent individualized good-cause findings. Title 15 CCR § 3574 (effective April 17, 2025) mandates CTMs at specified intervals, written good-cause findings on CDCR Form 3043, delivery of Form 3043 within 30 days of CTM, treatment provider signatures, and separation of treatment completion from parole discharge as legally separate decisions (§ 3574(f)(1)). CDCR, through employees Dodd, Doe 1, Noguchi, and Rojo, breached these mandatory duties by applying fabricated eligibility criteria, using an obsolete form, omitting required CTM documentation and good-cause findings, misidentifying Plaintiff in the review, and failing to serve Plaintiff with the decision. CDCR is vicariously liable under Gov. Code § 815.2(a) and directly liable under Gov. Code § 815.6. (Haggis v. City of Los Angeles (2000) 22 Cal.4th 490, 498; State of California v. Superior Court (Kellogg) (1997) 15 Cal.4th 1055, 1064–65.)

SECOND CAUSE OF ACTION — False Imprisonment against All Defendants: The parole supervision conditions (24/7 GPS monitoring, curfew, geographic restrictions, mandatory treatment, warrantless searches, threat of reincarceration) constitute nonconsensual confinement without lawful privilege for an appreciable period. Lawful privilege is absent because continued custody rests on fabricated rules and a review identifying a different person by name. Dodd's November 19, 2025 signature was the proximate cause of continued unlawful restraint. Doe 1 and Noguchi fabricated and ratified the defective report; Rojo's failure to serve the report eliminated any opportunity to challenge. Each day of continued supervision from November 19, 2025 is a separately actionable violation; per diem liberty damages of no less than $100/day sought. (Fermino v. Fedco, Inc. (1994) 7 Cal.4th 701, 715; Sullivan v. County of Los Angeles (1974) 12 Cal.3d 710, 718–720.)

THIRD CAUSE OF ACTION — Intentional Infliction of Emotional Distress (IIED) against All Individual Defendants: Defendants engaged in extreme and outrageous conduct exceeding all bounds of decency by: applying fabricated eligibility rules and a 6.5-year minimum; conducting a review so devoid of individualized assessment that it named a different parolee; continuing practices judicially condemned in Dwyer (2023) after regulatory amendments were enacted; withholding notice to prevent challenge; and uncritically adopting an impossible treatment-completion standard. Dodd's conduct in finalizing the denial despite facial defects constituted at minimum reckless disregard for Plaintiff's liberty and emotional well-being. (Hughes v. Pair (2009) 46 Cal.4th 1035, 1050–1051; Alcorn v. Anbro Engineering, Inc. (1970) 2 Cal.3d 493, 498–499.)

FOURTH CAUSE OF ACTION — Negligence against All Defendants: Each Defendant owed Plaintiff a duty of care arising from custodial authority and mandatory duties under Penal Code §§ 3000, 3001, 3008, and Title 15 § 3574. Individual Defendants are personally liable under Gov. Code § 820(a). Discretionary immunity under Gov. Code § 820.2 does not apply because all breached duties are ministerial ('shall' language, specific conditions, no policy discretion). Dodd's particularized duties as final approving official included ensuring compliance, rejecting non-compliant reviews, implementing April 2025 amendments, and conducting meaningful individualized review. Doe 1, Noguchi, and Rojo each breached duties through: fabrication of eligibility criteria; failure to conduct individualized assessment; failure to aggregate mandatory scoring criteria; use of an obsolete form; failure to convene CTMs or complete Form 3043; ratifying a report identifying a different person; and failure to provide notice of the decision.

Continuing Tort Doctrine: Each day of ongoing unlawful custodial supervision from November 19, 2025 constitutes a new, separately actionable breach for all four causes of action, preserving timeliness (Breach of Mandatory Duty: 2 years per CCP § 335.1; False Imprisonment: 1 year per CCP § 340(c); IIED: 2 years per CCP § 335.1; Negligence: 2 years per CCP § 335.1).

Underground Regulation Prohibition (Gov. Code § 11340.5): The fabricated '6.5-year minimum supervision requirement' is an unpublished underground regulation prohibited by statute, rendering it void and unenforceable.

Punitive Damages (Civil Code § 3294): Individual Defendants Dodd, Doe 1, Noguchi, and Rojo are each subject to punitive and exemplary damages for willful, oppressive, and malicious conduct, including conscious disregard of Plaintiff's rights despite prior judicial condemnation of these practices. Punitive damages are not sought against CDCR (Gov. Code § 818).

Negligence Per Se: Violation of the mandatory duties imposed by Penal Code § 3001(a)(2) and Title 15 CCR § 3574 constitutes negligence per se, with individual liability governed by Gov. Code § 820(a).

Government Claims Act Compliance (Gov. Code §§ 900–915.4): Plaintiff timely presented a tort claim on March 21, 2026; deemed rejected by operation of law on May 5, 2026; two-year limitations period applies under Gov. Code § 945.6(a)(2) because no written notice of rejection was ever received.
evidence_cited: CDCR Form 1502-DR (Discharge Review Report) dated November 12, 2025, authored by Doe 1, reviewed by Noguchi on November 14, 2025, approved by Dodd on November 19, 2025 — attached as Exhibit B. Contains 'Eagan' copy-paste error, fabricated 6.5-year rule, mutually exclusive statutory frameworks, unaggregated scoring, and blank service fields.
Government tort claim filed with California Government Claims Program on March 21, 2026 — attached as Exhibit A.

In re Frederick Dwyer, No. SC-080355A (San Mateo Super. Ct. Mar. 20, 2023) — judicial order condemning CDCR's practice of requiring treatment 'for the entire term of parole, apparently without reference to the individualized performance of each parolee' — attached as Exhibit C.

Alliance for Constitutional  Laws v. Macomber, No. 23STCP02378 (Sacramento Super. Ct., December 2023) — order overruling CDCR's demurrer, holding that petition alleging these statewide policies states a valid cause of action for ordinary mandate under CCP § 1085 — attached as Exhibit D.

Centerline Drivers Wage Notice dated October 31, 2024 — documenting Plaintiff's active CDL qualifications, regular pay rate of $33.35/hour, and premium pay rate of $50.03/hour — attached as Exhibit E.
Plaintiff's W-2 records documenting lost CDL wages of no less than $109,408 for the years 2022–2024, measured against but-for earning capacity of $72,000 per year.

April 17, 2025 amendments to Title 15 CCR § 3574 — regulatory amendments mandating CTMs, CDCR Form 3043 (Rev. 11/24), written good-cause findings, separation of treatment completion from parole discharge, and providing that 'supervised persons may complete a  management program before discharge from parole.'
Plaintiff's parole supervision records reflecting Case Type PC 3000(b)(5)(B), supervision category 'SA' ( Active), CSRA Score 2 (Moderate), Stockton 2 GPS Parole Unit, Region N.
CDCR Form 3043 (Rev. 11/24) — the mandatory form required by April 2025 regulations, which was NOT used in the November 2025 discharge review (obsolete Form 1502-DR Rev. 01/22 was used instead).
Noguchi's internal risk assessment scoring Plaintiff as 'Stable 6/LSCMI 5' (Level 2 below-average risk) — internally contradicting his own retention recommendation.

Parole violation records showing violations (failure to attend treatment, failure to charge GPS) were resolved with verbal reprimands — the lowest possible sanction — and were not deemed serious enough to warrant revocation.

HOPE Psychotherapy, Inc. treatment records showing Plaintiff's advancement to Core Phase, Block 1, Module 4 of 7, and assignment to approximately seven different clinicians due to extraordinary staff turnover since December 2020.
procedural_significance: This is the initiating complaint in an unlimited civil damages action filed May 8, 2026 in Sacramento Superior Court (in pro per), naming CDCR and four individual state parole officials as defendants. It establishes the operative factual record and four causes of action (Breach of Mandatory Duty, False Imprisonment, IIED, Negligence) arising from a procedurally void November 19, 2025 parole discharge denial. Key procedural features: (1) the continuing-tort doctrine is invoked to preserve timeliness across all four causes of action, treating each day of ongoing unlawful supervision as a separate violation; (2) Government Claims Act compliance is documented by timely presentment on March 21, 2026 and deemed rejection on May 5, 2026; (3) both CDCR (vicarious and direct liability) and four individual defendants in their individual capacities (personal liability and punitive damages) are named, with claims structured to defeat discretionary immunity under Gov. Code § 820.2 by characterizing all breached duties as ministerial; (4) the damages framework is established including per diem liberty value ($100/day minimum), documented wage loss quantum ($109,408 for 2022–2024 plus $6,000/month ongoing), and compliance costs; (5) a predicate for punitive damages is laid by documenting defendants' constructive knowledge of prior judicial condemnation (Dwyer 2023, Macomber 2023) and the April 2025 regulatory amendments, supporting findings of willful, oppressive, and malicious conduct under Civil Code § 3294; and (6) venue is established in Sacramento County based on Dodd's official office location and the locus of the operative November 19, 2025 administrative act.
